title: Think Tanks in America By Thomas Medvetz University of Chicago Press. 2012. 324 pages. $32.50 cloth
abstract: Think tanks have fundamentally transformed politics, journalism, and intellectual life in the United States. Organizations such as the Brookings Institution, the Heritage Foundation, and the CATO Institute are well known by social scientists and political commentators, who have written about them for decades. In spite of this attention, Medvetz suggests provocatively, we do not even have a good definition of what a think tank is, nor do we have an adequate theory for why they are organized the way they are. Think Tanks in America sets out to answer these fundamental questions. In the process, it ­provides a fascinating history and social topology of the “space of think tanks”; an analysis of the role that think tanks have played in recent welfare legislation; and an intervention into debates about public intellectuals and public sociology. This is a landmark work of political sociology, which will be read for a long time.
